activity.name: Autopsy
activity.screen: First, examine if there is any black soot in the victim's mouth.

screen.sootBtn: There's soot in the victim's mouth
screen.noSootBtn: There's no soot in the victim's mouth

activity.screen: To open the body, follow the marks with your scalpel to perform a Y-incision.
Next, remove the rib cage with the rib cutter to expose the inner organs.
Now, use the scalpel to open the trachea.

screen.sootBtn: There's soot in the victim's trachea
screen.noSootBtn: There's no soot in the victim's trachea

activity.screen: Lastly, we need to take a blood sample. Use the syringe to draw blood from the heart.

screen.text:
Once carbon monoxide enters the bloodstream, it binds to the hemoglobin in the red blood cells.
Click on a red blood cell to zoom in.
screen.caption: Blood vessel

screen.text:
Each red blood cell contains thousands of hemoglobin molecules. If hemoglobin doesn’t contain oxygen, it is called deoxyhemoglobin, or deoxyHb.
Click a deoxyhemoglobin molecule to proceed.
screen.caption: Red blood cell
Deoxyhemoglobin (deoxyHb)

screen.text:
Hemoglobin functions as the oxygen carrier in the blood. Oxygen molecules (O<sub>2</sub>) that are absorbed into the blood from the lungs bind to deoxyhemoglobin, which is then called oxyhemoglobin (O<sub>2</sub> Hb).
Click on the O2 button to start the process.
screen.caption: Deoxyhemoglobin (deoxyHb)

screen.text:
However, if a person inhales carbon monoxide (CO), the oxygen that is bound to oxyhemoglobin will be replaced with carbon monoxide, and carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) is formed.
Click on the CO button to release carbon monoxide.
screen.caption: Deoxyhemoglobin (deoxyHb)
Oxyhemoglobin (O<sub>2</sub>Hb)

screen.text: The reason that hemoglobin prefers carbon monoxide over oxygen is that hemoglobin has a greater chemical AFFINITY, or attraction, to carbon monoxide.
screen.caption: Deoxyhemoglobin (deoxyHb)
Oxyhemoglobin (O<sub>2</sub>Hb)
Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)

screen.text:
The human body tolerates a small amount of carboyhemoglobin (COHb). Higher percentages, however, are toxic or even deadly.
Use the yellow slider to increase the CO concentration.
screen.caption: COHb (%)
Status of hemoglobin in blood
Consequence
screen.result: Severe headache, dim vision, dizziness, nausea, irritability, heart arrhythmia
